---
title: "Data, plots and descriptive statistics"
subtitle: "Lecture 2"
date: 2026-03-24
webr:
  packages:
    - tidyverse
    - palmerpenguins
    - gt
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Data {background-color="#8e61aa"}

```{webr}
#| edit: false
#| echo: false
#| output: false
options("readr.edition" = 1)
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 20, base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible") + theme(aspect.ratio = 1))
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(gt)
library(ggiraph)

theme_set(
  theme_bw(base_size = 20, base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible") +
    theme(aspect.ratio = 1)
)
```

## Data

### Palmer penguins

![](images/lter_penguins.png)

## Data {.scrollable}

### Palmer penguins (observations)

```{r}
obs_table <- function(data, font_size = 22) {
  data |>
    gt() |>
    opt_row_striping() |>
    tab_options(
      table.font.size = font_size,
      table.font.names = "Atkinson Hyperlegible"
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
      locations = cells_column_labels()
    )
}

penguins |> obs_table()
```

## Data

### Palmer penguins (variables)

```{r}
penguin_vars <-
  tibble(
    variable = names(penguins),
    description = c(
      "penguin species (Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo)",
      "island where the penguin was found (Biscoe, Dream, Torgersen)",
      "length of bill in mm",
      "depth of bill in mm",
      "length of flipper in mm",
      "body mass in grams",
      "penguin sex (female, male)",
      "year of observation (2007, 2008, 2009)"
    )
  )

vars_table <- function(data, font_size = 30) {
  data |>
    gt() |>
    opt_row_striping() |>
    cols_label(
      variable = md("**Variable**"),
      description = md("**Description**")
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(style = "italic"),
      locations = cells_body(columns = variable)
    ) |>
    tab_options(
      table.font.size = font_size,
      table.font.names = "Atkinson Hyperlegible"
    )
}

penguin_vars |> vars_table()
```

## Data

### Dataframes

- Spreadsheet like arrangement of rows and columns
- When a single row is a unique case (piece of colleted data) = *tidy data*

```{r}
penguins |>
  slice_sample(n = 6) |>
  obs_table()
```

## Data

### Tidy data

![](images/tidydata.png)

- Each variable gets its own column
- Each observation gets its own row
- Each value gets its own cell
- Each new unit of observation gets its own data file

## Data

### Discussion questions

How would you organise the following data:

- You measure *enzyme activity* in different tissues (liver, kidney, muscle) from mice at *three* time points (0h, 24h, 48h). Each tissue sample from each mouse is measured *three* times (to check accuracy), with *absorbance values* recorded at 450nm and 600nm.

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Data

### Discussion questions

How would you organise the following data:

- You conduct bird surveys across multiple sites over several months. For each survey, you record the *number of individuals of different species seen*, the *time spent surveying*, *weather conditions* (temperature, wind, cloud cover), and *habitat characteristics* (% tree cover, distance to water).

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Data

### Discussion questions

How would you organise the following data:

- You collect health data from participants in an exercise intervention including their *age*, *height*, *weight* at baseline, *weight* after 6 months, *blood pressure* at baseline (systolic/diastolic), *blood pressure* after 6 months (systolic/diastolic), and whether they completed the *intervention* (yes/no).

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

# Variable types {background-color="#8e61aa"}

## Variable types

### What is a variable?

- A variable is any property that is measured in an observation [@sokal1995]
- Something that **varies** among things that we can measure [@dytham2011]
- Usually not selected at random, i.e., we should have some interest in it
- Can be related to both a treatment and response in an experiment

## Variable types

### What can we do with variables?

- Summarise how our measurements vary using summary statistics
- Visualise our measurements with figures
- Predict one variable using a second variable
  - The variable we want to predict: **response variable** or **dependant variable**
  - The variable we use to predict our response variable: **explanatory variable** or **independant variable**

## Variable types

### Palmer penguins (variables)

```{r}
penguin_vars_extra <-
  penguin_vars |>
  add_row(
    variable = "cuteness",
    description = "subjective cuteness rating (very low, low, average, high, very high)"
  ) |>
  add_row(
    variable = "parasites",
    description = "number of parasites observed"
  )

penguin_vars_extra |>
  vars_table(font_size = 24)
```

## Variable types

### Palmer penguins (observations)

How would you group these variables? Which are similar kinds of data, and which are different? How many groups would you make?

```{r}

penguins_extra <-
  penguins |>
  mutate(
    size_score = scale(body_mass_g)[, 1],
    cuteness = case_when(
      size_score < -1.5 ~ "very high",
      size_score < -0.5 ~ "high",
      size_score < 0.5 ~ "average",
      size_score < 1.5 ~ "low",
      TRUE ~ "very low"
    ),
    cuteness = factor(
      cuteness,
      levels = c("very low", "low", "average", "high", "very high")
    ),
    cuteness = if_else(
      row_number() %in% sample(n(), size = floor(n() * 0.05)),
      NA,
      cuteness
    ),
    parasites = rpois(n(), lambda = 3),
    parasites = if_else(
      row_number() %in% sample(n(), size = floor(n() * 0.05)),
      NA_integer_,
      parasites
    )
  ) |>
  select(-size_score)

penguins_extra |>
  slice_sample(n = 8) |>
  obs_table(font_size = 18)
```

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Variable types

### Categorical variables

- Have a fixed number of discrete values
- The measurement we take will assign our observation to a specific value
- E.g., species (*Adelie*, *Chinstrap*, *Gentoo*)
- Can be either **nominal** or **ordinal**
  - **Nominal** variables *do not* have inherent order *(e.g., species)*
  - **Ordinal** variables *do* have an inherent order *(e.g., cuteness)*

## Variable types

### Quantitative variables

- Represented by numbers that reflect a magnitude
- Unlike categorical variables, the numbers we collect mean something tangible
- Can be either **discrete** or **continuous**:
  - **Discrete** variables can only take certain values *(e.g., parasites (1, 2, 3, etc), not 1.55)*
  - **Continuous** variales can take any real number (a number that can be represented by a decimal) within some range. *(e.g., bill length or mass)*

# Exploratory data analysis{background-color="#8e61aa"}

- Quantitative data
- Categorical data

## Exploratory data analysis

### Visualising a single variable

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Variable: body mass of Adelie penguins (grams)
- X axis mapped to body mass
- How best to visualise?

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| warning: false

penguins_adelie <-
  penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie")

bm_dot <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)") +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  )

bm_dot
```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Visualising a single variable

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Variable: body mass of Adelie penguins (grams)
- X axis mapped to body mass
- How best to visualise?

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
bm_dot + geom_point(aes(y = 0), fill = "grey25", size = 5) + ylim(0, 10)
```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Visualising a single variable

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Variable: body mass of Adelie penguins (grams)
- X axis mapped to body mass
- How best to visualise?

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
bm_dot +
  geom_point(aes(y = 0), size = 5, fill = "grey25") +
  ylim(0, 10) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = seq(
      min(penguins_adelie$body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
      max(penguins_adelie$body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
      by = 100
    ),
    linewidth = 0.5,
    colour = "red"
  )

```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Visualising a single variable: **dotplot**

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- A dotplot is a graphical representation of the distribution of a dataset
- Each observation is represented by a single dot, stacked vertically within bins
- The height of each stack shows the frequency of observations within that range

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| warning: false

bm_dot +
  geom_dotplot(
    binwidth = 100,
    colour = "white",
    fill = "grey25",
    method = "histodot"
  )
```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Visualising a single variable: **histogram**

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- A histogram is a graphical representation of the distribution of a dataset
- It divides the data into bins (intervals) and displays the frequency of data points in each bin
- The height of each bar represents the number of observations within each bin

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| warning: false

bm_hist <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 18, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)")


bm_hist
```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Measures of central tendancy: **the (arithmetic) mean**

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n}
$$
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

```{webr}
(6 + 8 + 6 + 2 + 9 + 9 + 7 + 9) / 8
```

```{webr}
mean(c(6, 8, 6, 2, 9, 9, 7, 9))
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| warning: false
bm_hist +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE), y = 0, fill = "Mean"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(name = "Statistics", values = c("Mean" = "#a53ce6")) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.8))
```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Measures of central tendancy: **the median**

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- The middle piece of data

```{webr}
data <- c(6, 8, 6, 2, 9, 9, 7, 9)
sort(data)
```

```{webr}
median(data)
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| warning: false
bm_hist +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE), y = 0, fill = "Mean"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE), y = 0, fill = "Median"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Statistics",
    values = c("Mean" = "#a53ce6", "Median" = "#e63946")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.8))
```

:::
::::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Measures of central tendancy

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

```{webr}
penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    mean = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

:::
::: {.column}

```{r}
penguin_hist_all <-
  penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 18, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  facet_wrap(
    vars(species),
    ncol = 1,
    strip.position = "left",
    scales = "free_y"
  ) +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)", y = "Count") +
  theme(
    aspect.ratio = 0.33,
    strip.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  )

penguin_hist_all +
  geom_point(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        body_mass_g = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    aes(x = body_mass_g, y = 0, fill = "Mean"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        body_mass_g = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    aes(x = body_mass_g, y = 0, fill = "Median"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Statistics",
    values = c("Mean" = "#a53ce6", "Median" = "#e63946")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9))
```

:::
:::

## Exploratory data analysis

### Measures of spread

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

```{webr}

penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

:::
::: {.column}

```{r}
penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ) |>
      tidyr::pivot_longer(c(min, max), names_to = "stat", values_to = "x"),
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(min = "#1f77b4", max = "#d62728"),
    breaks = c("min", "max"),
    labels = c("Min", "Max"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

:::
:::

## Descriptive statistics & plots

### Measures of spread: **the range**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

- Highly sensitive to outliers

$$
\text{Range} = \text{Max}(x) - \text{Min}(x)
$$

```{webr}
penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(range = max - min)
```

:::
::: {.column}

```{r}
penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ) |>
      tidyr::pivot_longer(c(min, max), names_to = "stat", values_to = "x"),
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    mapping = aes(x = min, xend = max, y = 5, yend = 5),
    arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.1, "inches")),
    colour = "green",
    linewidth = 1,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(min = "#1f77b4", max = "#d62728"),
    breaks = c("min", "max"),
    labels = c("Min", "Max"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

```

:::
:::

## Descriptive statistics & plots

### Measures of spread: **quartiles**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

:::
::: {.column}

```{r}
quartiles_df <- penguins |>
  summarise(
    q1 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    q3 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = species
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(q1, median, q3), names_to = "stat", values_to = "x")

penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = quartiles_df,
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(q1 = "#1f77b4", median = "#e63946", q3 = "#2ca02c"),
    labels = c(q1 = "25th", median = "Median", q3 = "75th"),
    name = "Quartiles"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9))
```

:::
:::

# Extracting meaning from data {background-color="#8e61aa"}

## Extracting meaning from data

### Relationships between variables

- Many research questions wish to answer if two variables are related:
  - Does tree spacing affect timber yield?
  - Does egg mass affect offspring survival?
  - Do face masks reduce transmission of SARS-CoV-2?
  - Do larger *Adelie* penguins also have longer bills?

## Extracting meaning from data {.scrollable}

### Relationships between variables

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

```{r}

penguins_extra |>
  filter(species == "Adelie") |>
  select(species, body_mass_g, bill_length_mm) |>
  obs_table(font_size = 18)

```

:::
::: {.column}

- Do larger *Adelie* penguins also have longer bills?

:::
:::

## Extracting meaning from data

### Relationships between variables

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
adelie_scatter <-
  penguins_extra |>
  filter(species == "Adelie") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  labs(
    x = "Body Mass (g)",
    y = "Bill Length (mm)"
  )

adelie_scatter
```

:::
::: {.column}

- Do larger *Adelie* penguins also have longer bills?

:::
:::

## Extracting meaning from data

### Relationships between variables

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

```{r}
adelie_scatter + geom_point(alpha = 0.7)
```

:::
::: {.column}

- Do larger *Adelie* penguins also have longer bills?
- What is the shape of the relationship?
- Is the relationship weak or strong?

{{< countdown minutes=1.5 font-size="5rem" >}}

:::
:::

## References
